cards.text: The insertion operator {{c1::<<}} is used with std::cout, while the extraction operator {{c2::>>}} is used with std::cin.
I/O operators must be implemented as {{c1::friend}} or {{c2::normal}} functions (not member functions) because the left operand is a {{c3::stream object}}, not your class.

cards.question: What does this operator<< overload do?
cards.answer: Overloads the insertion operator to output a Point object to a stream. Returns the stream by reference to allow chaining.
cards.explanation: Takes std::ostream& (the stream) and const Point& (object to output). Returns out by reference for chaining (e.g., std::cout << p1 << p2).

cards.front: Why must operator<< and operator>> return a reference to the stream?
cards.back: Returning a stream reference allows chaining multiple operations together, e.g., std::cout << a << b << c. Each operator returns the stream, which becomes the left operand for the next operation.

cards.text: For operator<<, the first parameter is {{c1::std::ostream&}} and the second is {{c2::const YourClass&}}. For operator>>, the first is {{c3::std::istream&}} and the second is {{c4::YourClass&}} (non-const).

cards.question: What's wrong with this operator>> implementation?
cards.answer: The Point parameter is const, but operator>> needs to modify the object to read into it. It should be Point& (non-const reference).
cards.explanation: Extraction operator must modify the object to store input values, so the parameter cannot be const.

cards.front: Should operator<< take its object parameter by const reference or non-const reference?
cards.back: const reference. The insertion operator only reads from the object to output it, so it should not modify the object. This also allows outputting temporary objects and const objects.

cards.explanation:
Standard pattern for I/O operators:
  • operator<<: std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const T& obj)
  • operator>>: std::istream& operator>>(std::istream& in, T& obj)
  • Both return stream reference for chaining
  • Typically implemented as friend functions
  • Output operator takes const reference, input takes non-const
examples: friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const Point& p);
friend std::istream& operator>>(std::istream& in, Point& p);

cards.front: Why can't operator<< be implemented as a member function?
cards.back: Because the left operand is std::ostream, not your class. Member functions require the left operand to be an object of your class (*this). You'd need std::cout.operator<<(myObj), which requires modifying the standard library.

cards.text: To enable chaining like std::cout << a << b, operator<< must return {{c1::std::ostream&}} (a reference to the output stream).

cards.question: How would you use this operator>> overload?
cards.answer: std::cin >> myPoint; or you can chain: std::cin >> p1 >> p2;
cards.explanation: The operator reads two values from the input stream into the Point's x and y members, allowing standard input syntax.
